title: Project Proposal
text:
Oceanus Folk is a music genre that gained global recognition largely due to the success of Oceanus artist Sailor Shift. Beginning her career in the early 2020s as a member of the all-female band Ivy Echoes, Sailor later pursued a solo career and achieved a major breakthrough in 2028 when one of her singles reached the top of the global charts. Since then, she has continued to grow in popularity while collaborating with artists across multiple genres.
As Sailor’s influence expanded, attention also grew around Oceanus Folk and its impact on the wider music ecosystem. To study this phenomenon, journalist Silas Reed compiled a knowledge graph dataset containing information about artists, albums, songs, collaborations, and musical influences.
However, the interconnected nature of this dataset makes it difficult to analyze using traditional methods. This project aims to develop an interactive visual analytics application using Tableau that allows Silas to explore Sailor Shift’s career, her collaborations, and the broader spread of Oceanus Folk within the music industry.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Oceanus Folk is a music genre that gained global recognition largely due to the success of Oceanus artist Sailor Shift. Beginning her career in the early 2020s as a member of the all-female band Ivy Echoes, Sailor later pursued a solo career and achieved a major breakthrough in 2028 when one of her singles reached the top of the global charts. Since then, she has continued to grow in popularity while collaborating with artists across multiple genres.
As Sailor’s influence expanded, attention also grew around Oceanus Folk and its impact on the wider music ecosystem. To study this phenomenon, journalist Silas Reed compiled a knowledge graph dataset containing information about artists, albums, songs, collaborations, and musical influences.
However, the interconnected nature of this dataset makes it difficult to analyze using traditional methods. This project aims to develop an interactive visual analytics application using Tableau that allows Silas to explore Sailor Shift’s career, her collaborations, and the broader spread of Oceanus Folk within the music industry.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Motivations
Influence in the music industry is shaped by complex relationships between artists, genres, and collaborations. These relationships often form interconnected networks that are difficult to interpret using static charts.
The rise of Oceanus Folk presents a compelling case of how a regional genre can gain global recognition. With Sailor Shift at the center of this transformation, understanding how influence spreads through collaborations and musical inspiration can provide insights into the evolution of the genre.
Visual analytics provides an effective approach for exploring such complex relationships by enabling interactive analysis of large interconnected datasets. Through interactive visualizations, Silas Reed will be able to investigate how Oceanus Folk evolved, how Sailor Shift influenced other artists, and how the genre continues to develop.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Objectives
The objective of this project is to develop a Tableau-based visual analytics system that enables Silas Reed to explore the rise of Sailor Shift and the growing influence of Oceanus Folk within the global music landscape. The system will support exploration of the following questions relevant to Silas Reed’s article Oceanus Folk: Then-and-Now:

Who has influenced Sailor Shift over the course of her career
Which artists she has collaborated with and who she has influenced in return
How Oceanus Folk has spread across the broader music world
Whether the influence of Oceanus Folk expanded gradually or through key breakthrough periods
Which genres and artists have been most influenced by Oceanus Folk
How Oceanus Folk itself has evolved with Sailor Shift’s rise
What patterns characterize artists who emerge as rising stars in the music industry
How the careers of selected artists compare in terms of popularity and influence

title: Project Proposal
text:
Scope of Work
This project focuses on building an interactive Tableau application that allows exploration of the dataset compiled by Silas Reed. The work will involve three main stages:

Data Preparation The dataset will be examined and structured to support analysis of relationships between artists, songs, genres, collaborations, and influences.
Visualization Development Interactive visualizations will be created to support exploration of artist influence, collaboration patterns, genre relationships, and artist career trajectories.
Insight Generation The visualizations will be used to analyze patterns related to Sailor Shift’s career and the spread of Oceanus Folk, helping Silas Reed develop insights for his article Oceanus Folk: Then-and-Now.

title: Data Wrangling
text:
Data Wrangling
The graph-structured data (nodes + edges) needed to be reshaped into flat tables for Tableau to consume, and into filtered subgraphs for Gephi network visualisation. Two Python scripts handle these respectively.
No data was deleted, modified, imputed, or fabricated. All values in the output tables originate directly from the source files.

Tableau Wrangling (mc1_wrangle.py)

Approach: Normalised Bridge Tables
The Tableau wrangling script adopts a normalised bridge table architecture rather than producing a single large flattened table per question. This approach is safer for Tableau because it avoids row duplication caused by many-to-many relationships (e.g. a song with multiple performers and multiple influences would otherwise multiply rows unpredictably).
The pipeline consists of four stages:
1. Node normalisation — All node IDs and date fields (release_date, written_date, notoriety_date) are cast to integer types. A display_name field is derived from stage_name where available, falling back to name. Boolean flags (is_work, is_person_or_group, is_oceanus_folk) are added to each node for efficient downstream filtering.
2. Edge normalisation — Source and target IDs are cast to integers, and a key column is added for multigraph disambiguation.
3. Edge enrichment — A single enriched edge table is built by joining node attributes onto both the source and target of every edge (equivalent to two SQL LEFT JOINs). All subsequent tables are derived from this one enriched table, ensuring consistency.
4. Table construction — Specialised functions derive each output table from the enriched edge table using aggregation, pivoting, and filtering.


Operations Performed







Operation
Description




Filter
Select rows matching criteria (e.g. genre = “Oceanus Folk”, influence-type edges, Sailor Shift’s edges)


Join / Enrich
Attach node attributes (name, genre, release year) to both ends of every edge in a single enriched table — equivalent to two SQL LEFT JOINs


Pivot / Unstack
Reshape role counts (PerformerOf, ComposerOf etc.) from long to wide format for the works master table


Aggregate
Count occurrences such as number of performers per work, influence references per song, and collaborators per artist


Derive
Compute new fields — display_name from stage name, career_span_years = last − first release year, is_oceanus_folk_artist flag, draws_from_count and influenced_others_count per work


Classify
Assign directional labels to influence edges — "Sailor -> Other" vs "Other -> Sailor", and "OF -> Other" vs "Other -> OF" vs "OF -> OF"





Key Entities Identified
Through filtering, the following key entities were identified for analysis:

Sailor Shift (Person, id=17255) — the central figure of the investigation
Sailor Shift’s works — 46 works linked to Sailor Shift either directly (via her own PerformerOf, ComposerOf, or LyricistOf edges) or indirectly (via her group memberships — e.g. Ivy Echos — through MemberOf edges). The v1 script captured only 38 works by missing group membership involvement; the revised script correctly captures all 46.
Ivy Echos (MusicalGroup) — Sailor Shift’s primary band, found via a MemberOf edge from Sailor Shift’s node
Oceanus Folk catalogue — 305 works filtered by genre = “Oceanus Folk”





Gephi Wrangling (gephi_prep.py)
A separate script prepares filtered subgraphs for Gephi network visualisation, one per research question. All outputs are artist-only where possible (Person and MusicalGroup nodes only) to maximise readability. Each question produces a pair of CSV files — _nodes.csv and _edges.csv — importable directly into Gephi.
Column conventions across all Gephi outputs:







Column
Description




Id / Label
Gephi-required node identifier and display name


Source / Target
Gephi-required directed edge endpoints


EdgeType
The original relationship type from the raw data (renamed from Type to avoid Gephi’s reserved word conflict)


Relationship
Assigned role label for node/edge colouring in Gephi (Q2 and Q3 only)


EdgeCategory
Two-value field (influence / creation) for simplified edge colouring (Q1 extended only)


Weight
Number of Sailor’s songs referencing a given artist (Q1 artists only)




Q1a — Who has Sailor Shift been most influenced by?
Five influence edge types are used throughout: InStyleOf, InterpolatesFrom, DirectlySamples, CoverOf, LyricalReferenceTo. MemberOf is excluded — membership is not equivalent to influence.
Four graph levels are produced, designed to be used together as a layered narrative:









Output
Nodes
Edges
What it shows




q1a
69
77
Most granular — Sailor’s own songs as intermediary nodes, with creation and influence edges


q1a_clean
31
30
Mid level — Sailor directly connected to the songs/albums she drew from


q1a_extended
125
154
With creator context — adds the artists who made those influenced songs; edges tagged with EdgeCategory (influence / creation)


q1a_artists
99
98
Clearest — Sailor directly to artists only, no song/album nodes; Weight = number of her songs referencing that artist



Key finding: Sailor Shift’s influences are entirely songs and albums — no artist directly influenced her at the person level except through direct edges (InStyleOf, DirectlySamples). The 99 artists in q1a_artists are the creators of the works she drew from, resolved via a two-hop collapse.


Q1b — Who has she collaborated with and directly or indirectly influenced?
All Q1b output is artist-only (Person/MusicalGroup), saved as a single file pair: q1b_artists_nodes.csv / q1b_artists_edges.csv. Three relationship types are encoded in the Relationship node column for colour-coding in Gephi:








Relationship
Count
Definition




Collaborator
48
Shared song credits on Sailor’s songs


DirectlyInfluenced
7
Artists/groups with influence edges pointing TO Sailor herself


IndirectlyInfluenced
4
Person/MusicalGroup artists who reference collaborators’ songs (RecordLabels excluded)












Output
Nodes
Edges
What it shows




q1b_artists
60
59
Sailor directly connected to all three groups, tagged by Relationship



Key finding: Nobody references Sailor’s own songs directly in the dataset. Her direct influenced artists are all groups (Cassette Future, Silver Veil, The Phantom Operators, The Hollow Monarchs, Copper Canyon Ghosts) or individuals (Cassian Storm, Claire Holmes) who point to Sailor herself via influence edges.


Q1c — How has she influenced collaborators of the broader Oceanus Folk community?
All Q1c output is artist-only (Person/MusicalGroup), saved as a single file pair: q1c_artists_nodes.csv / q1c_artists_edges.csv. Node roles are encoded in the Relationship column:








Relationship
Count
Definition




SailorShift
1
Sailor Shift


CollabOFArtist
11
Sailor’s collaborators who are also Oceanus Folk artists — the bridge group


OFCommunity
3
OF artists reached via shared songs with bridge collaborators


DirectlyInfluenced
1
Copper Canyon Ghosts — the only OF artist that directly references Sailor












Output
Nodes
Edges
What it shows




q1c_artists
16
45
Sailor → bridge collaborators → OF community, with CollaboratedWith and SharedOFSong edge types



Key finding: Sailor Shift’s influence on Oceanus Folk is entirely indirect. No Oceanus Folk songs reference her songs. Her footprint exists through 11 collaborators who are also OF artists, who in turn worked on 34 OF songs and connected to 3 further OF artists. The graph is intentionally small (16 nodes) — this accurately reflects the limited but traceable indirect influence path.

title: Data Wrangling
text:
Output Tables

Tableau Outputs (mc1_wrangle.py)
The wrangling produces 9 Tableau-ready CSV files across two tiers: four normalised base tables that can be joined in Tableau’s data source editor, and five pre-aggregated summary tables ready to use directly in worksheets.

Base Tables (join in Tableau)

works_master.csv (4,611 rows × 24 columns)
A single flat record for every Song and Album in the dataset. Contributor names (performers, composers, lyricists, producers) and label names are aggregated into semicolon-delimited strings per work, with separate numeric count columns for filtering. Influence counts (draws_from_count, influenced_others_count) are pre-joined from the influence bridge.
Key columns: id, work_name, work_type, genre, release_year, written_year, notoriety_year, notable, single, is_oceanus_folk, performers, composers, lyricists, producers, num_performers, num_composers, num_lyricists, num_producers, recorded_by_labels, distributed_by_labels, draws_from_count, influenced_others_count


contributors_bridge.csv (29,882 rows × 11 columns)
One row per contributor–work relationship, covering all PerformerOf, ComposerOf, LyricistOf, ProducerOf, RecordedBy, and DistributedBy edges. A role_group column distinguishes creative contributors from label relationships.
Key columns: edge_type, role_group, source_id, source_display_name, source_type, work_id, work_name, work_genre, work_release_year


influence_bridge.csv (7,407 rows × 13 columns)
One row per influence relationship across the entire dataset, covering all five influence edge types. Both source and target node attributes are fully resolved.
Key columns: influence_type, source_id, source_display_name, source_genre, source_year, target_id, target_display_name, target_genre, target_year


memberof_bridge.csv (568 rows × 8 columns)
One row per person–group membership, with both person and group attributes fully resolved.
Key columns: person_id, person_display_name, person_type, group_id, group_display_name, group_type



Summary Tables (use directly in Tableau)

sailor_shift_career_v2.csv (46 rows × 32 columns)
Sailor Shift’s full discography with additional columns describing her specific involvement, distinguishing direct contributions from group membership involvement (resolves an undercounting issue in v1 which missed 8 group-membership works).
Additional columns over works_master: sailor_involvement_modes, sailor_roles, sailor_linked_groups, is_direct_person_work, is_group_membership_work, collaborators, num_distinct_collaborators


sailor_shift_influences_v2.csv (40 rows × 14 columns)
All influence edges involving Sailor Shift or her works, with a direction column classifying each edge as "Sailor -> Other" (32 edges) or "Other -> Sailor" (8 edges).


oceanus_folk_spread_v2.csv (601 rows × 15 columns)
All influence edges touching at least one Oceanus Folk work, with direction classifying each as OF -> Other, Other -> OF, or OF -> OF.


oceanus_folk_year_summary.csv (40 rows × 5 columns)
Year-level summary of the Oceanus Folk genre for timeline visualisations.
Key columns: year, of_works_released, of_notable_works, of_to_other_influence_edges, other_to_of_influence_edges


artist_careers_v2.csv (1,370 rows × 16 columns)
Career profiles for all artists and musical groups with 3 or more contributed works, including performance counts, genre breadth, career span, and influence reception score.




Gephi Outputs (gephi_prep.py)









File pair
Nodes
Edges
Question




q1a_nodes / q1a_edges
69
77
Q1a — Full song-level influence graph


q1a_clean_nodes / q1a_clean_edges
31
30
Q1a — Sailor to influenced songs/albums


q1a_extended_nodes / q1a_extended_edges
125
154
Q1a — With artist creators of influenced songs


q1a_artists_nodes / q1a_artists_edges
99
98
Q1a — Sailor to artists only


q1b_artists_nodes / q1b_artists_edges
60
59
Q1b — Collaborators and influenced artists


q1c_artists_nodes / q1c_artists_edges
16
45
Q1c — Sailor’s bridge into Oceanus Folk

text:
Changes from Previous Version
The revised script differs from the original in the following ways:








Aspect
Previous version
Revised version




Library
Python standard library only (csv, collections)
pandas


Architecture
One flat table per question
Normalised bridge tables + summary tables


Sailor Shift’s works
38 works (direct edges only)
46 works (direct edges + group membership)


Influence direction
String label "Influenced Sailor Shift" / "Sailor Shift Influenced"
Standardised "Sailor -> Other" / "Other -> Sailor"

Two separate rows for each direction
Single row with direction column (OF -> Other, Other -> OF, OF -> OF)

—
works_master.csv, contributors_bridge.csv, influence_bridge.csv, memberof_bridge.csv, oceanus_folk_year_summary.csv


display_name
Not present
Derived from stage_name, falling back to name

title: Tableau Story
text:
This page walks users through the interactive Tableau story developed for the Oceanus Folk: Then-and-Now project. The story complements the static poster by allowing users to explore the temporal spread of Oceanus Folk and the characteristics of rising stars through interactive dashboards.
The Tableau story is designed to answer the following project questions:

Was Oceanus Folk’s influence intermittent or did it have a gradual rise?
What genres and top artists have been most influenced by Oceanus Folk?
How has Oceanus Folk changed with the rise of Sailor Shift, and from which genres does it now draw contemporary inspiration?
How do three artists compare in their rise in popularity and influence?
Who are the next likely Oceanus Folk stars over the next five years?

title: Tableau Story
text:
This page walks users through the interactive Tableau story developed for the Oceanus Folk: Then-and-Now project. The story complements the static poster by allowing users to explore the temporal spread of Oceanus Folk and the characteristics of rising stars through interactive dashboards.
The Tableau story is designed to answer the following project questions:

Was Oceanus Folk’s influence intermittent or did it have a gradual rise?
What genres and top artists have been most influenced by Oceanus Folk?
How has Oceanus Folk changed with the rise of Sailor Shift, and from which genres does it now draw contemporary inspiration?
How do three artists compare in their rise in popularity and influence?
Who are the next likely Oceanus Folk stars over the next five years?

title: Tableau Story
text:
How to Use This Page
Use the embedded Tableau story below to explore the dashboards directly on the website.

Click through each story point using the navigation controls in Tableau.
Hover over marks to view detailed values, years, genres, and artist names.
Compare patterns across charts rather than reading each graph in isolation.
Refer to the insight notes below each section for the key interpretations.
Use the poster image further below for a quick static summary of the full project.

title: Tableau Story
text: Interactive Tableau Story

title: Tableau Story
text:
Story Walkthrough and Analytical Insights

1. Evolution and Global Spread of Oceanus Folk
This part of the Tableau story addresses how Oceanus Folk spread across time, artists, and genres. Rather than showing a smooth upward line, the dashboards reveal a more uneven pattern of growth.

Was the rise gradual or intermittent?
The rise of Oceanus Folk appears to be intermittent rather than gradual.

Growth happens in distinct bursts, not as a steady year-on-year climb.
The genre shows long quieter periods, especially in its earlier years, before sharp spikes emerge.
Major surges suggest that Oceanus Folk spread through key moments of adoption and influence, rather than constant organic expansion.
In the more recent years, outward influence becomes stronger, suggesting that Oceanus Folk has moved from absorbing inspiration to exporting its own sound.


Insight: Oceanus Folk does not expand in a straight line; it grows in waves, with certain periods acting as turning points in the genre’s wider musical influence.



What genres and artists were most influenced by Oceanus Folk?
The dashboards suggest that Oceanus Folk’s impact is broadly distributed across multiple artists and nearby genres.

Influence is not concentrated in just one breakout artist.
Several artists show visible uptake, indicating that Oceanus Folk diffused across a wider ecosystem rather than passing through a single dominant successor.
Genre-level patterns point especially to Desert Rock, Indie Folk, and Dream Pop as key areas of influence.
This implies that Oceanus Folk resonates most strongly with genres that share atmospheric sound, emotional storytelling, and reflective tone.


Insight: Oceanus Folk acts less like a niche genre with one heir and more like a stylistic foundation that multiple artists and adjacent genres build upon.



How has Oceanus Folk changed with the rise of Sailor Shift?
The Tableau story also shows that Oceanus Folk has changed internally as it spread outward.

Contemporary Oceanus Folk is no longer shaped only by traditional folk roots.
Newer inspiration appears to come from genres such as Synthwave and Space Rock, alongside more familiar folk-adjacent sounds.
This points to a genre that is becoming more hybrid, atmospheric, and experimental over time.
Sailor Shift’s rise seems to coincide with this broadening identity, suggesting that her success may have helped normalize genre blending within the Oceanus Folk space.


Insight: With the rise of Sailor Shift, Oceanus Folk evolves from a rooted folk tradition into a more hybrid soundscape that blends organic origins with modern atmospheric and electronic influences.




2. Defining and Predicting Rising Stars
This section of the Tableau story shifts from genre diffusion to artist profiling. It looks at what a rising star trajectory tends to look like and which artists seem best positioned to shape the next phase of Oceanus Folk.

What does a rising star look like?
The visualisations suggest that a rising star is not defined by one sudden viral moment alone.

Strong candidates tend to show consistent notable output over time.
Their trajectories suggest sustained momentum, not just a single isolated spike.
They often sit close to current genre trends, especially where Oceanus Folk overlaps with adjacent styles like Dream Pop or Indie Folk.
In other words, rising stars appear to be artists who are both productive and well-positioned within an evolving sound landscape.


Insight: A rising star is not just an artist with attention, but one whose output, timing, and stylistic fit place them at the centre of the genre’s next phase.



Comparing three artist trajectories
The three artist career paths in the story show that there is no single formula for musical rise.

One artist may peak early and then plateau.
Another may rise gradually but maintain stronger long-term stability.
A third may break through later, but accelerate rapidly once momentum begins.

This comparison matters because it shows that influence can emerge in different ways. However, artists with steady build-up and repeated notable works often appear more durable than those relying on one short-lived spike.

Insight: Different artists rise differently, but sustained momentum tends to matter more than a one-off breakthrough when predicting long-term influence.



Who are the next Oceanus Folk stars?
Based on the prediction view, the strongest next-wave candidates are the artists with both high scores and strong recent work patterns.

Bea Azure and Kael Storm stand out as the clearest frontrunners.
Copper Canyon Group also performs strongly and remains close behind.
These artists appear aligned with the dominant contemporary directions of Oceanus Folk, which strengthens the plausibility of their future rise.
Their profiles suggest that future stars are not random outliers, but artists who already fit the genre’s current momentum and evolution.


Insight: The next Oceanus Folk stars are the artists who combine strong recent output with stylistic alignment to where the genre is already heading.

title: Tableau Story
text:
Poster Summary
The poster below provides a static overview of the project findings and can be used as a quick visual summary before or after exploring the Tableau story.

title: Tableau Story
text:
Key Takeaways
From the Tableau story, users should come away with five main insights:

Oceanus Folk spread through bursts of influence, not smooth gradual growth.
Its impact is visible across multiple artists and adjacent genres, rather than being concentrated in a single successor.
The genre has evolved into a more hybrid and experimental form, especially with the rise of Sailor Shift.
Rising stars tend to show sustained momentum, repeated notable work, and alignment with current genre direction.
Artists such as Bea Azure, Kael Storm, and Copper Canyon Group appear best positioned to shape the next phase of Oceanus Folk.

title: Tableau Story
section: Suggested Caption for Navigation or Homepage
text:
Suggested Caption for Navigation or Homepage
Explore the Tableau Story — interact with the dashboards behind Oceanus Folk’s spread, see how the genre changed over time, and discover which artists are likely to lead its next chapter.

title: Tableau Story User Guide
text:
This user guide explains how to navigate the Tableau story for the Oceanus Folk: Then-and-Now project. The story was built to help users explore Sailor Shift’s influence, the spread of Oceanus Folk, and the identification of rising stars through interactive dashboards.



The Tableau story is designed to support the following tasks:

understand Sailor Shift’s musical influences and collaboration patterns,
examine how Oceanus Folk spread across artists and genres over time,
compare artists by influence, career trajectory, and prediction score,
identify the most likely next-generation Oceanus Folk stars.




When the Tableau story opens, users can move from one story point to the next using the navigation tabs or arrows provided in Tableau.
Each story point presents a different analytical angle. Users should move through the story in sequence for the clearest narrative flow, though individual dashboards can also be explored independently.





Hover over marks, nodes, bars, and points to reveal detailed values such as artist names, counts, years, and influence totals.



Clicking a mark may highlight related data or filter other views on the same dashboard, depending on the interaction that has been configured.



Some dashboards include filters that allow users to narrow the analysis by artist, year, genre, or relationship type. Use these to focus on a particular part of the story.



If the view becomes too filtered or highlighted, use Tableau’s reset or revert options to return to the default dashboard state.






Start here to understand the project context, the role of Sailor Shift, and the purpose of the analysis.



Use this section to understand why the dataset is difficult to interpret through conventional tables and why visual analytics is useful.



Focus on the artist network views and supporting charts. These reveal how Sailor Shift was influenced, who she worked with, and how her influence spread through relationships.



Read this section to understand how the genre changed over time, when growth accelerated, and what external genres shaped its modern form.



Use the final section to compare artist trajectories and identify who is most likely to become a future leader in Oceanus Folk.






This section should be interpreted as a network story rather than a simple popularity chart. A central position does not automatically mean direct control; it may instead indicate bridge-building through collaborations and community ties.



This section is best read as a timeline of bursts rather than a steady upward trend. Spikes suggest key moments when the genre absorbed outside influence or began exporting its style more strongly.



Prediction scores should be treated as indicators, not guarantees. They help identify promising artists based on current patterns in the data.





Read the annotations and captions before interacting with a dashboard.
Use one filter at a time if you want to isolate the effect of a specific variable.
Reset the dashboard before switching to a new question.
Compare the Tableau story with the project poster for both interactive and static perspectives.






Check that the Tableau Public link is correct and that the workbook is publicly accessible.



Increase the iframe height in the website embed code or use Tableau’s fullscreen mode.



Use Tableau’s revert button or refresh the page to restore the original state.




Need help using the Tableau story? Open the dashboards in order, hover for detailed values, use filters sparingly, and reset the view whenever needed. For the clearest experience, follow the story from Sailor Shift’s network to Oceanus Folk’s spread and then to the rising stars analysis.

This section is best read as a timeline of bursts rather than a steady upward trend. Spikes suggest key moments when the genre absorbed outside influence or began exporting its style more strongly.



Prediction scores should be treated as indicators, not guarantees. They help identify promising artists based on current patterns in the data.

title: Tableau Story User Guide
text: Need help using the Tableau story? Open the dashboards in order, hover for detailed values, use filters sparingly, and reset the view whenever needed. For the clearest experience, follow the story from Sailor Shift’s network to Oceanus Folk’s spread and then to the rising stars analysis.

Characterise rising stars and predict the next Oceanus Folk stars

title: Visual Analytics Story
text:
How to Use This Page

Start with the Gephi sections to understand Sailor Shift’s direct and indirect network relationships.
Move to the embedded Tableau story to explore the time-based and predictive dashboards.
Hover over marks in Tableau to see detailed values, artist names, years, and genres.
Use the poster at the end as a quick static summary of the overall project.

title: Visual Analytics Story
section: Part 1: Profiling Sailor Shift’s Career Influence Network
text:
Part 1: Profiling Sailor Shift’s Career Influence Network

Q1A. Who has Sailor Shift been most influenced by over time?
 
Sailor Shift’s musical DNA draws from a wide web of artists, predominantly solo performers (93.94%) with a few key musical groups like Ivy Echos, Phantom Roots, and The Fiddle & The Fjord. The dominant influence type is InStyleOf (35.71%), suggesting SS absorbed the sound and aesthetic of her influences rather than directly copying them. CoverOf edges (25.51%) show she frequently reinterpreted others’ works, while InterpolatesFrom (18.37%) and DirectlySamples (9.18%) indicate she also wove fragments of existing music into her own compositions. LyricalReferenceTo connections (11.22%) reveal a storytelling tradition — she didn’t just borrow sound, she referenced narratives. The network reveals a densely connected influence ecosystem radiating inward toward SS, with artists like Ivy Echos and Drowned Harbor serving as notable hubs.


Q1B. Who has she collaborated with and directly or indirectly influenced?
 
Sailor Shift’s outward reach is heavily collaboration-driven — 67.61% of her connections are Collaborator edges, showing she built relationships through joint work rather than passive influence alone. Musical groups feature more prominently here (21.67%) compared to her influences, with names like Tidal Reverie, The Wave Riders, The Brine Choir, and Violet Engines forming key collaborative hubs. Her IndirectlyInfluenced connections (22.54%) far outweigh DirectlyInfluenced ones (9.86%), meaning SS’s impact rippled outward through intermediaries rather than through direct stylistic imitation — a hallmark of a genre-shaping artist whose influence spreads organically through the community.


Q1C. How has she influenced collaborators and the broader Oceanus Folk community?
 
This tightly clustered network shows SS’s position within the core Oceanus Folk ecosystem — an even 50/50 split between Persons and MusicalGroups. The overwhelming majority of edges are OFCommunity ties (73.33%), representing the genre’s internal fabric of shared musical identity. CollabOFArtist connections (24.44%) show artists who collaborated directly with OF musicians, while DirectlyInfluenced edges are rare (2.22%), reinforcing that SS’s impact on the broader community operates through collaboration and community membership rather than top-down influence. The dense interconnections among nodes like Ella Parker, Tidal Reverie, Mariner’s Refrain, and Sophie Ramirez — with SS sitting at the periphery connecting outward to Copper Canyon Ghosts — suggest SS acts as a bridge between the tight-knit OF core and artists outside the genre.

title: Visual Analytics Story
text: Part 2: Interactive Tableau Story

title: Visual Analytics Story
text:
Part 3: Tableau Insights
Click through the Tableau story sheets to follow along for each question and insights.

Q2. How has Oceanus Folk spread through the musical world?

Was the influence intermittent or gradual?
Based on the blue line, which represents Oceanus Folk’s influence on other genres and artists, its growth is intermittent rather than gradual, occurring in distinct bursts. After a relatively dormant period in the 1990s, Oceanus Folk begins to exert very slight outward influence during the 2004–2006 wave, followed by a stronger and more sustained expansion from the late 2010s into the 2020s, specifically spiking in 2013. Throughout the 2020s to 2030s, Ocenaus Folk’s influence remained consistent with a slight peak in influence every 2 years. This can be seen from the peaks in 2020, 2023, 2026 and 2029. But a decline continues from 2030-2040, showing how Oceanus Folks lose influence during those years.
In contrast, the orange line, which captures influence from other genres onto Oceanus Folk, shows an earlier and sharper spike around 2017–2018, peaking at over 61 connections. This indicates a phase where Oceanus Folk was primarily absorbing external influences.
Together, the two lines reveal a clear progression: Oceanus Folk initially develops by drawing heavily from other genres (orange), before transitioning into a phase where it increasingly exports its own influence outward (blue). This highlights a shift from influence recipient to influence driver, reinforcing the idea that the genre evolves through cycles of absorption and dissemination.


What genres and artists were most influenced by Oceanus Folk?
The genre exchange graph highlights how Oceanus Folk participates in a two-way flow of influence with other genres. On one hand, Oceanus Folk draws heavily from genres such as Indie Folk, Dream Pop, and Desert Rock, as seen from the strong inward influence. On the other hand, it also contributes back to these same genres, indicating that influence is not one-directional. This reflects a process of genre exchange, where Oceanus Folk continuously absorbs stylistic elements and reinterprets them, rather than simply imitating or dominating other genres.
Oceanus Folk’s outward influence is most evident in genres like Indie Folk, Synthwave, and Dream Pop, which show the highest levels of adoption. At the artist level, influence is widely distributed, with Fixx’d leading with 3 works, followed by Lei Jin, Na Ren, Yang Wan, and Yong Shen at 2 each. The spread across multiple artists suggests that Oceanus Folk functions as a shared creative foundation, rather than being concentrated within a single adopter. Together, this reinforces the idea of genre exchange, where influence is diffused across a network of genres and artists rather than flowing in a single direction.


How has Oceanus Folk changed with the rise of Sailor Shift?
With the rise of Sailor Shift, Oceanus Folk has undergone a clear transformation in both its influences and impact over time. As seen in the earlier timeline graph, the 2020–2030 surge in Oceanus Folk’s outward influence closely aligns with Sailor Shift’s rise in career, suggesting that her emergence played a key role in accelerating the genre’s expansion.
At the same time, Oceanus Folk continues to draw from contemporary genres such as Desert Rock, Indie Folk, and Dream Pop, with additional influences from Synthwave and Space Rock, indicating a shift toward more atmospheric and electronic sounds. Together, this reflects a genre that not only evolves by absorbing diverse inspirations, but also grows in prominence through Sailor Shift’s influence, transitioning into a more hybrid and outward-driving musical force.



Q3. What does it mean to be a rising star in the music industry?

What defines a rising star profile?
A rising star in the music industry is characterised less by longevity and more by rapid accumulation of notable works within a short career span. The graph shows that artists like Sailor Shift fall within the “fast risers” category, achieving above-median output early in their careers, distinguishing them from early-stage artists who have yet to gain traction and established stars who grow more gradually over time.
This suggests that rising stars are defined by early breakout momentum, where influence and recognition scale quickly rather than steadily. Overall, a rising star profile reflects an artist who can accelerate impact in a compressed timeframe, positioning themselves for long-term influence even before reaching peak career maturity.


Comparing the trajectories of three artists
The career trajectories of Sailor Shift, Yang Wan, and The Brine Choir highlight distinct paths to popularity and influence. Sailor Shift exemplifies a fast-rising trajectory, achieving a rapid concentration of notable works shortly after her breakthrough, signalling strong and early influence. In contrast, Yang Wan represents a more established and sustained growth pattern, with influence built steadily over a longer career span. The Brine Choir, meanwhile, reflects a slower and less concentrated rise, with fewer notable works and a more gradual progression.


Who are the next Oceanus Folk stars?
Using this characterization, rising stars are best defined by early breakout momentum combined with sustained output, rather than longevity alone. Based on this profile and predictive scoring, Bea Azure, Kael Storm, and Copper Canyon Ghosts emerge as the most likely next Oceanus Folk stars. These artists not only have the highest prediction scores, but also demonstrate strong recent activity and alignment with the fast-riser trajectory, positioning them to lead the genre’s influence over the next five years.



Bonus Insight: Sailor Shift’s Genre Expansion
Sailor Shift’s career trajectory shows a clear shift from focused beginnings in Oceanus Folk to progressive genre diversification over time. Following her breakthrough around 2028, there is a noticeable increase in both the volume and spread of works across genres such as Synthwave and Americana. This suggests that her influence is not confined to a single genre, but expands outward as her career matures, reinforcing her role as a key driver of Oceanus Folk’s evolution into a more hybrid and cross-genre sound.

title: Visual Analytics Story
text:
Poster Summary
The poster below provides a static summary of the full project and can be used as a quick overview before or after exploring the interactive story.

title: Visual Analytics Story
text:
Key Takeaways

Sailor Shift evolves through interconnected networks of influence, not isolated artists
Influence is reciprocal and collaborative, as shown by Gephi network structures
Oceanus Folks’ growth is burst-driven rather than gradual, with key inflection points over time
The genre transitions from absorbing influence → exporting influence
Sailor Shift acts as a catalyst, accelerating both genre spread and transformation
Oceanus Folk reflects genre exchange, blending elements across multiple styles
Influence is widely distributed, not concentrated in a single artist or genre
Rising stars are defined by early breakout momentum + sustained output
Future stars align with current genre trends and high activity levels
Overall, Oceanus Folk functions as a dynamic ecosystem of shared and evolving influence
